evidence: Package structure is consistent: api/, config/, exploration/, indexer/blacklab/, model/, query/, utils/. Each package has a focused domain. No directories follow different organizational patterns.
Java naming conventions are uniformly applied: camelCase methods, PascalCase types, SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE constants throughout all files.

Access modifiers in the api package follow a consistent pattern: WordSketchApiServer is public, all handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, VisualizationHandlers, ExploreResponseBuilder, HttpApiUtils) are package-private. This correctly encapsulates the handler layer.

MultiSeedExplorer is package-private (no modifier) while CollocateProfileComparator is public class, both in the exploration/ package. Both are implementation classes for the SemanticFieldExplorer facade. There is no documented reason why one is public and the other is not.

Model class design strategy is intentionally mixed: ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult are traditional classes with get-prefixed accessors; AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationResult are records with component accessors. The package-info.java explicitly documents this: 'traditional classes use get-prefixed accessors; records omit the prefix per Java record conventions.' The rationale is stated and the split is intentional.

Deprecated constructors/overloads follow a consistent pattern across both SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator: @Deprecated annotation, same-class delegation, Javadoc @deprecated note with removal timeline and preferred alternative. This is systematic.
Abbreviation 'colloc' (truncated) vs 'collocate' (full) in related exploration files — covered under naming_quality but also a convention outlier across sibling modules.

handleSemanticFieldComparison in ExplorationHandlers builds its response inline (direct HashMap puts) while handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti delegate to ExploreResponseBuilder.populateExploreResponse. Sibling handlers in the same class follow different response-assembly conventions.
BCQL endpoint uses 'limit' parameter name in the request body; GET sketch endpoints use 'top'. Both control max-results but use different names — a cross-endpoint naming drift.

strengths: Java naming conventions (case, visibility) are uniformly applied with zero drift — no camelCase/snake_case mixing, no inconsistent constant formats.
Package organization has a clear, documented separation of concerns with no files in surprising locations (after the recent RadialPlot/LongIntHashMap moves).
The mixed record/class model design is explicitly documented in package-info.java with a stated rationale.
Deprecated API evolution follows a consistent two-overload pattern with versioned removal notes in both places it appears.
convention_outlier.issue_character: Issues are isolated convention drifts within the exploration package and the API response layer — one public/package-private inconsistency, one inline vs. delegated response-building inconsistency — rather than file-organization or naming-convention drift.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase largely follows consistent conventions. The score is pulled below 80 by two concrete outliers: CollocateProfileComparator being public while its sibling MultiSeedExplorer is package-private (without documented rationale), and handleSemanticFieldComparison building its response inline while its three sibling handlers in the same class delegate to ExploreResponseBuilder — a visitor to that class will see three handlers that look one way and one handler that looks another way, which is the definition of a style island.

issues.summary: CollocateProfileComparator is public while sibling MultiSeedExplorer is package-private — no documented reason for the access asymmetry
evidence: 'public class CollocateProfileComparator' — publicly accessible outside the exploration package.
Both are implementation classes behind SemanticFieldExplorer. Neither is part of a documented public API.
No Javadoc or comment explains why CollocateProfileComparator is public when MultiSeedExplorer is not.
issues.suggestion: If CollocateProfileComparator is not part of the intended public API, make it package-private to match MultiSeedExplorer. If it is intentionally public (e.g. for direct use by tests or extensions), add a Javadoc note explaining the decision.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldComparison builds its response inline while all three sibling exploration handlers delegate to ExploreResponseBuilder
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti calls the same ExploreResponseBuilder methods.

handleSemanticFieldComparison builds its response entirely inline: direct 'response.put(...)' calls, and calls ExploreResponseBuilder.formatAdjectiveProfile and ExploreResponseBuilder.buildEdges from within the handler itself.
The divergence means response shape for the comparison endpoint is owned in a different layer than the explore endpoints.
issues.suggestion: Move comparison response construction into ExploreResponseBuilder as a new 'populateComparisonResponse(Map, ComparisonResult)' method, matching the delegation pattern of the other handlers.

root_causes: handleSemanticFieldComparison was likely implemented before ExploreResponseBuilder was introduced as a dedicated response-building layer.
CollocateProfileComparator may have been made public to allow direct test instantiation before test helpers existed.
